title: 〜は〜です (topic + copula)
pattern: N は N/Adj です
meaning: X is Y — identifies or describes the topic
explanation: The は particle marks the sentence topic. です is the polite copula 'is/am/are'. This is the most fundamental Japanese sentence structure.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"これは本です。","reading":"これはほんです。","english":"This is a book."},{"japanese":"田中さんは学生です。","reading":"たなかさんはがくせいです。","english":"Tanaka-san is a student."},{"japanese":"私は日本人です。","reading":"わたしはにほんじんです。","english":"I am Japanese."}]

title: 〜は〜じゃありません (negative copula)
pattern: N は N/Adj じゃありません
meaning: X is not Y — polite negative identification
explanation: じゃありません (or ではありません in formal writing) is the polite negative form of です. Used to deny or contradict a statement about the topic.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"これは本じゃありません。","reading":"これはほんじゃありません。","english":"This is not a book."},{"japanese":"私は先生じゃありません。","reading":"わたしはせんせいじゃありません。","english":"I am not a teacher."},{"japanese":"田中さんは学生じゃありません。","reading":"たなかさんはがくせいじゃありません。","english":"Tanaka-san is not a student."}]

title: 〜でした / じゃありませんでした (past copula)
pattern: N/Adj でした / じゃありませんでした
meaning: Was / was not — past tense of the copula
explanation: でした is the polite past affirmative copula. じゃありませんでした is the polite past negative. Used to describe past states or identities.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"昨日は休みでした。","reading":"きのうはやすみでした。","english":"Yesterday was a holiday."},{"japanese":"子供の頃、元気じゃありませんでした。","reading":"こどものころ、げんきじゃありませんでした。","english":"I was not energetic as a child."},{"japanese":"彼は学生でした。","reading":"かれはがくせいでした。","english":"He was a student."}]

title: 〜が (subject marker)
pattern: N が V/Adj
meaning: Marks the grammatical subject, often with focus or new information
explanation: が marks the subject of a sentence and often carries a sense of emphasis or introduces new information. Contrasts with は which marks a known topic.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"猫がいます。","reading":"ねこがいます。","english":"There is a cat."},{"japanese":"雨が降っています。","reading":"あめがふっています。","english":"It is raining."},{"japanese":"誰が来ましたか。","reading":"だれがきましたか。","english":"Who came?"}]

title: 〜を (object marker)
pattern: N を V
meaning: Marks the direct object of a transitive verb
explanation: を marks what the action of the verb is directed at. It is always followed by a transitive verb and cannot be used with intransitive verbs.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"水を飲みます。","reading":"みずをのみます。","english":"I drink water."},{"japanese":"本を読みました。","reading":"ほんをよみました。","english":"I read a book."},{"japanese":"音楽を聴きます。","reading":"おんがくをききます。","english":"I listen to music."}]

title: 〜に (location / time / destination)
pattern: N に V (ある/いる/行く/来る etc.)
meaning: Marks location of existence, point in time, or destination
explanation: に has multiple uses: it marks the location where something exists (with あります/います), a specific point in time, or the destination of movement verbs like 行く and 来る.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"机の上に本があります。","reading":"つくえのうえにほんがあります。","english":"There is a book on the desk."},{"japanese":"三時に来てください。","reading":"さんじにきてください。","english":"Please come at three o'clock."},{"japanese":"学校に行きます。","reading":"がっこうにいきます。","english":"I go to school."}]

title: 〜で (location of action / means / reason)
pattern: N で V / N で N
meaning: Marks the place where an action occurs, the means used, or scope
explanation: で marks where an action takes place (contrasting with に which marks existence), the tool or means used to do something, or the scope within which something applies.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"図書館で勉強します。","reading":"としょかんでべんきょうします。","english":"I study at the library."},{"japanese":"バスで学校に行きます。","reading":"バスでがっこうにいきます。","english":"I go to school by bus."},{"japanese":"日本語で話してください。","reading":"にほんごではなしてください。","english":"Please speak in Japanese."}]

title: 〜へ (direction)
pattern: N へ V (行く/来る/帰る etc.)
meaning: Marks direction or destination of movement (interchangeable with に for destination)
explanation: へ indicates the direction toward which movement is heading. It emphasizes the direction rather than arrival, and is often interchangeable with に for destination but sounds slightly more literary.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"東京へ行きます。","reading":"とうきょうへいきます。","english":"I am going to Tokyo."},{"japanese":"家へ帰りました。","reading":"いえへかえりました。","english":"I went home."},{"japanese":"日本へようこそ。","reading":"にほんへようこそ。","english":"Welcome to Japan."}]

title: 〜と (and / with / quotation)
pattern: N と N / N と V / V と言う
meaning: Connects nouns exhaustively ('and'), marks a companion ('with'), or introduces a quote
explanation: と connects nouns as an exhaustive list (listing all items, unlike や which is partial). It also marks who an action is done together with, and introduces direct or indirect quotations.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"りんごとオレンジを買いました。","reading":"りんごとオレンジをかいました。","english":"I bought apples and oranges."},{"japanese":"友達と映画を見ました。","reading":"ともだちとえいがをみました。","english":"I watched a movie with a friend."},{"japanese":"彼は「行きます」と言いました。","reading":"かれは「いきます」といいました。","english":"He said 'I will go'."}]

title: 〜の (possessive / noun modifier)
pattern: N の N
meaning: Indicates possession, affiliation, or modifies the following noun
explanation: の links two nouns, showing that the first noun modifies or belongs to the second. It can indicate possession, material, category, or other attributive relationships.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"これは私の本です。","reading":"これはわたしのほんです。","english":"This is my book."},{"japanese":"日本語の先生はどこですか。","reading":"にほんごのせんせいはどこですか。","english":"Where is the Japanese teacher?"},{"japanese":"東京の大学に行きたいです。","reading":"とうきょうのだいがくにいきたいです。","english":"I want to go to a university in Tokyo."}]

title: 〜も (also / too / even)
pattern: N も V/Adj
meaning: Indicates 'also' or 'too', adding the marked item to what was already stated
explanation: も replaces は, が, or を to mean 'also' or 'too'. When used with negatives it means 'either' (〜もありません = there isn't one either). It can also mean 'even' for emphasis.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"私も日本語を勉強しています。","reading":"わたしもにほんごをべんきょうしています。","english":"I am also studying Japanese."},{"japanese":"コーヒーも紅茶もあります。","reading":"コーヒーもこうちゃもあります。","english":"There is both coffee and tea."},{"japanese":"彼女も来ませんでした。","reading":"かのじょもきませんでした。","english":"She didn't come either."}]

title: 〜か (question particle)
pattern: S か
meaning: Turns a sentence into a yes/no question
explanation: か added at the end of a sentence with rising intonation forms a polite question. In casual speech, か alone can sound blunt, so か with ね or a rising tone is common.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"これは本ですか。","reading":"これはほんですか。","english":"Is this a book?"},{"japanese":"明日来ますか。","reading":"あしたきますか。","english":"Are you coming tomorrow?"},{"japanese":"日本語が話せますか。","reading":"にほんごがはなせますか。","english":"Can you speak Japanese?"}]

title: 〜ね / 〜よ (sentence-final particles)
pattern: S ね / S よ
meaning: ね seeks agreement ('right?'); よ asserts new information ('you know')
explanation: ね is used when seeking confirmation or sharing a feeling with the listener. よ asserts information the speaker believes the listener doesn't know yet. Both soften the directness of a statement.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"いい天気ですね。","reading":"いいてんきですね。","english":"It's nice weather, isn't it?"},{"japanese":"このケーキはおいしいですよ。","reading":"このケーキはおいしいですよ。","english":"This cake is delicious, you know."},{"japanese":"明日は休みですね。","reading":"あしたはやすみですね。","english":"Tomorrow is a holiday, right?"}]

title: Verb ます form (polite non-past)
pattern: V-ます
meaning: Polite present/future affirmative verb form
explanation: The ます form is the standard polite verb ending used in most daily conversation. It can express habitual actions, general truths, or future intentions depending on context.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"毎日学校に行きます。","reading":"まいにちがっこうにいきます。","english":"I go to school every day."},{"japanese":"明日映画を見ます。","reading":"あしたえいがをみます。","english":"I will watch a movie tomorrow."},{"japanese":"日本語を話します。","reading":"にほんごをはなします。","english":"I speak Japanese."}]

title: Verb ません form (polite negative)
pattern: V-ません
meaning: Polite present/future negative verb form
explanation: ません is the polite negative non-past ending. It negates the action described by the verb. ませんか is also used to make polite invitations or suggestions.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"お酒を飲みません。","reading":"おさけをのみません。","english":"I don't drink alcohol."},{"japanese":"日曜日は学校に行きません。","reading":"にちようびはがっこうにいきません。","english":"I don't go to school on Sundays."},{"japanese":"分かりません。","reading":"わかりません。","english":"I don't understand."}]

title: Verb ました / ませんでした (polite past)
pattern: V-ました / V-ませんでした
meaning: Polite past affirmative and negative verb forms
explanation: ました is the polite past affirmative. ませんでした is the polite past negative. Both express completed actions or states in the past.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"昨日本を読みました。","reading":"きのうほんをよみました。","english":"I read a book yesterday."},{"japanese":"朝ごはんを食べませんでした。","reading":"あさごはんをたべませんでした。","english":"I didn't eat breakfast."},{"japanese":"映画は面白かったですか。","reading":"えいがはおもしろかったですか。","english":"Was the movie interesting?"}]

title: V て form (te-form)
pattern: V-て / V-で
meaning: Connects actions sequentially or serves as base for many grammatical structures
explanation: The て-form connects two or more actions in sequence. It is also the base for many important constructions: て+います, て+ください, て+もいい, etc. Its form varies by verb group.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"手を洗って、ご飯を食べます。","reading":"てをあらって、ごはんをたべます。","english":"I wash my hands and eat rice."},{"japanese":"ドアを開けて、入ってください。","reading":"ドアをあけて、はいってください。","english":"Please open the door and come in."},{"japanese":"起きて、シャワーを浴びました。","reading":"おきて、シャワーをあびました。","english":"I got up and took a shower."}]

title: 〜ています (ongoing action / current state)
pattern: V-て + います
meaning: Expresses an ongoing action or a resulting state that continues
explanation: て+います has two core meanings: an action currently in progress (I am doing X), or a state that results from a past action and continues (the window is open). Context determines which applies.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"今、テレビを見ています。","reading":"いま、テレビをみています。","english":"I am watching TV now."},{"japanese":"窓が開いています。","reading":"まどがあいています。","english":"The window is open."},{"japanese":"彼は東京に住んでいます。","reading":"かれはとうきょうにすんでいます。","english":"He lives in Tokyo."}]

title: い-adjective + です
pattern: Adj-い です
meaning: Polite sentence ending with an い-adjective
explanation: い-adjectives end in い and directly precede nouns or conclude sentences. Adding です makes the sentence polite. The い is part of the adjective, not added—e.g., 高い is already complete.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"この映画は面白いです。","reading":"このえいがはおもしろいです。","english":"This movie is interesting."},{"japanese":"今日は寒いですね。","reading":"きょうはさむいですね。","english":"It's cold today, isn't it?"},{"japanese":"そのケーキは甘いです。","reading":"そのケーキはあまいです。","english":"That cake is sweet."}]

title: な-adjective + です
pattern: Adj-な です (drop な before です)
meaning: Polite sentence ending with a な-adjective
explanation: な-adjectives (also called nominal adjectives) require な when directly modifying a noun (静かな部屋) but drop it before です (部屋は静かです). They conjugate differently from い-adjectives.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"この公園は静かです。","reading":"このこうえんはしずかです。","english":"This park is quiet."},{"japanese":"彼女はとても親切です。","reading":"かのじょはとてもしんせつです。","english":"She is very kind."},{"japanese":"日本語の勉強は大変ですか。","reading":"にほんごのべんきょうはたいへんですか。","english":"Is studying Japanese hard?"}]

title: い-adjective negative (〜くないです)
pattern: Adj-い → Adj-くないです
meaning: Polite negative form of い-adjectives
explanation: To negate an い-adjective, drop い and add くないです (or くありません in more formal speech). いい becomes よくない, not いくない — this is the one irregular case.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"今日はあまり寒くないです。","reading":"きょうはあまりさむくないです。","english":"It's not very cold today."},{"japanese":"この映画は面白くないです。","reading":"このえいがはおもしろくないです。","english":"This movie is not interesting."},{"japanese":"この問題は難しくないです。","reading":"このもんだいはむずかしくないです。","english":"This problem is not difficult."}]

title: い-adjective past (〜かったです)
pattern: Adj-い → Adj-かったです / くなかったです
meaning: Past tense of い-adjectives (was / was not)
explanation: To form the past of an い-adjective, drop い and add かったです (affirmative) or くなかったです (negative). いい becomes よかったです in the past.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"昨日は寒かったです。","reading":"きのうはさむかったです。","english":"It was cold yesterday."},{"japanese":"映画はあまり面白くなかったです。","reading":"えいがはあまりおもしろくなかったです。","english":"The movie wasn't very interesting."},{"japanese":"テストは難しかったですか。","reading":"テストはむずかしかったですか。","english":"Was the test difficult?"}]

title: な-adjective past (〜でした / じゃなかったです)
pattern: Adj-な → でした / じゃなかったです
meaning: Past tense of な-adjectives (was / was not)
explanation: な-adjectives use でした for the past affirmative and じゃなかったです (or ではありませんでした formally) for the past negative. They conjugate like nouns with the copula.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"その部屋は静かでした。","reading":"そのへやはしずかでした。","english":"That room was quiet."},{"japanese":"子供の頃は元気じゃなかったです。","reading":"こどものころはげんきじゃなかったです。","english":"I wasn't energetic as a child."},{"japanese":"試験は大変でしたか。","reading":"しけんはたいへんでしたか。","english":"Was the exam tough?"}]

title: これ / それ / あれ / どれ (demonstrative pronouns)
pattern: これ / それ / あれ / どれ
meaning: This / that (near listener) / that (far) / which — stand-alone demonstrative pronouns
explanation: これ refers to something near the speaker, それ to something near the listener, あれ to something far from both. どれ asks 'which one' among three or more options.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"これは何ですか。","reading":"これはなんですか。","english":"What is this?"},{"japanese":"それは私のかばんです。","reading":"それはわたしのかばんです。","english":"That is my bag."},{"japanese":"あれはきれいですね。","reading":"あれはきれいですね。","english":"That (over there) is beautiful, isn't it?"}]

title: この / その / あの / どの + N (demonstrative adjectives)
pattern: この/その/あの/どの + N
meaning: This / that / that (far) / which — demonstratives that directly modify nouns
explanation: Unlike これ/それ/あれ which stand alone, この/その/あの/どの must directly precede a noun. The distance rules are the same: この = near speaker, その = near listener, あの = far from both.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"この本は面白いです。","reading":"このほんはおもしろいです。","english":"This book is interesting."},{"japanese":"その映画を見ましたか。","reading":"そのえいがをみましたか。","english":"Did you see that movie?"},{"japanese":"あの山は高いです。","reading":"あのやまはたかいです。","english":"That mountain is tall."}]

title: ここ / そこ / あそこ / どこ (location demonstratives)
pattern: ここ / そこ / あそこ / どこ
meaning: Here / there (near listener) / over there / where
explanation: ここ, そこ, あそこ refer to locations using the same speaker/listener/far distance system as other demonstratives. どこ asks about location ('where?').
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"駅はここです。","reading":"えきはここです。","english":"The station is here."},{"japanese":"トイレはどこですか。","reading":"トイレはどこですか。","english":"Where is the restroom?"},{"japanese":"あそこに座りましょう。","reading":"あそこにすわりましょう。","english":"Let's sit over there."}]

title: 〜から〜まで (from ... to ...)
pattern: N/時間 から N/時間 まで
meaning: From [point] to [point] — marking a range in time or space
explanation: から marks the starting point and まで marks the ending point. They can be used for time (9 o'clock to 5 o'clock) or place (Tokyo to Osaka). They can be used together or separately.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"9時から5時まで働きます。","reading":"くじからごじまではたらきます。","english":"I work from 9 o'clock to 5 o'clock."},{"japanese":"東京から大阪まで新幹線で行きます。","reading":"とうきょうからおおさかまでしんかんせんでいきます。","english":"I go from Tokyo to Osaka by Shinkansen."},{"japanese":"月曜日から金曜日まで学校があります。","reading":"げつようびからきんようびまでがっこうがあります。","english":"There is school from Monday to Friday."}]

title: 〜たい (want to do)
pattern: V-stem + たい
meaning: Expresses the speaker's desire to do something
explanation: たい attaches to the verb stem to express a first-person desire. It conjugates like an い-adjective (たくない, たかった). It should not be used to describe a third person's desire without qualification.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"日本に行きたいです。","reading":"にほんにいきたいです。","english":"I want to go to Japan."},{"japanese":"何を食べたいですか。","reading":"なにをたべたいですか。","english":"What do you want to eat?"},{"japanese":"早く家に帰りたいです。","reading":"はやくいえにかえりたいです。","english":"I want to go home soon."}]

title: 〜ましょう / 〜ましょうか (let's / shall we?)
pattern: V-stem + ましょう / ましょうか
meaning: Proposes a shared action ('let's do') or offers to do something ('shall I?')
explanation: ましょう is a volitional form expressing 'let's do X together'. ましょうか softens it to a suggestion or offer, meaning 'shall we?' or 'shall I?'.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"一緒に行きましょう。","reading":"いっしょにいきましょう。","english":"Let's go together."},{"japanese":"昼ごはんを食べましょうか。","reading":"ひるごはんをたべましょうか。","english":"Shall we eat lunch?"},{"japanese":"始めましょう。","reading":"はじめましょう。","english":"Let's begin."}]

title: 〜ませんか (won't you? / invitation)
pattern: V-stem + ませんか
meaning: Politely invites the listener to do something together
explanation: ませんか is used to make polite invitations or suggestions. It is more tentative and polite than ましょう since it asks for the listener's preference rather than assuming agreement.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"一緒に映画を見ませんか。","reading":"いっしょにえいがをみませんか。","english":"Won't you watch a movie with me?"},{"japanese":"コーヒーを飲みませんか。","reading":"コーヒーをのみませんか。","english":"Would you like to have some coffee?"},{"japanese":"公園を散歩しませんか。","reading":"こうえんをさんぽしませんか。","english":"Shall we take a walk in the park?"}]

title: 〜てください (please do)
pattern: V-て + ください
meaning: Politely requests that someone do something
explanation: て+ください is a polite request form. It is used to ask someone to do an action. For more formal situations, the plain imperative or お〜ください can be used instead.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"ここに座ってください。","reading":"ここにすわってください。","english":"Please sit here."},{"japanese":"名前を書いてください。","reading":"なまえをかいてください。","english":"Please write your name."},{"japanese":"ゆっくり話してください。","reading":"ゆっくりはなしてください。","english":"Please speak slowly."}]

title: 〜ないでください (please don't)
pattern: V-ない + でください
meaning: Politely requests that someone not do something
explanation: ないで+ください is formed by attaching でください to the plain negative form. It politely asks someone to refrain from an action. More direct than the formal negative imperative.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"ここで写真を撮らないでください。","reading":"ここでしゃしんをとらないでください。","english":"Please don't take photos here."},{"japanese":"遅刻しないでください。","reading":"ちこくしないでください。","english":"Please don't be late."},{"japanese":"心配しないでください。","reading":"しんぱいしないでください。","english":"Please don't worry."}]

title: 〜が好き / 嫌い / 上手 / 下手 (likes and abilities)
pattern: N が 好き/嫌い/上手/下手 です
meaning: Like / dislike / good at / bad at — uses が to mark the object of feeling or skill
explanation: These expressions use が rather than を to mark their object. 好き and 嫌い express feelings toward something; 上手 and 下手 describe skill level. They are な-adjectives.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"音楽が好きです。","reading":"おんがくがすきです。","english":"I like music."},{"japanese":"料理が上手ですね。","reading":"りょうりがじょうずですね。","english":"You're good at cooking, aren't you?"},{"japanese":"虫が嫌いです。","reading":"むしがきらいです。","english":"I dislike insects."}]

title: 〜ができる (can do / be able to)
pattern: N が できる / V (plain) + ことができる
meaning: Expresses ability or possibility — can do something
explanation: できる can follow a noun (料理ができる = can cook) or こと+が to nominalize a verb phrase (話すことができる = can speak). The noun version is more natural in daily speech.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"日本語が少しできます。","reading":"にほんごがすこしできます。","english":"I can speak a little Japanese."},{"japanese":"料理をすることができません。","reading":"りょうりをすることができません。","english":"I cannot cook."},{"japanese":"早く走ることができますか。","reading":"はやくはしることができますか。","english":"Can you run fast?"}]

title: 〜にあります / います (existence)
pattern: N に N が あります / います
meaning: There is / there are — あります for inanimate objects, います for living beings
explanation: あります is used for non-living things and abstract objects. います is used for people, animals, and other animate beings. Both use に to mark the location of existence.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"机の上に本があります。","reading":"つくえのうえにほんがあります。","english":"There is a book on the desk."},{"japanese":"公園に子供がいます。","reading":"こうえんにこどもがいます。","english":"There are children in the park."},{"japanese":"冷蔵庫にジュースがありますか。","reading":"れいぞうこにジュースがありますか。","english":"Is there any juice in the fridge?"}]

title: 〜ごろ / ぐらい / くらい (approximately)
pattern: 時間/数量 + ごろ / ぐらい / くらい
meaning: Around / about — approximate time or quantity
explanation: ごろ is used for approximate points in time (around 3 o'clock). ぐらい/くらい indicate approximate quantities, durations, or degrees (about 3 hours, about 5 people). They are interchangeable.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"三時ごろ来てください。","reading":"さんじごろきてください。","english":"Please come around 3 o'clock."},{"japanese":"一時間ぐらいかかります。","reading":"いちじかんぐらいかかります。","english":"It takes about an hour."},{"japanese":"五人くらい来ると思います。","reading":"ごにんくらいくるとおもいます。","english":"I think about 5 people will come."}]

title: 〜や〜など (non-exhaustive list)
pattern: N や N など
meaning: Things like X and Y — lists items as examples, implying more exist
explanation: や connects nouns in a partial list, implying other items also exist (unlike と which is exhaustive). など adds 'and so on' or 'for example' at the end to make the non-exhaustive nature explicit.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"りんごやバナナなどを買いました。","reading":"りんごやバナナなどをかいました。","english":"I bought things like apples and bananas."},{"japanese":"日本語や中国語などが話せます。","reading":"にほんごやちゅうごくごなどがはなせます。","english":"I can speak languages like Japanese and Chinese."},{"japanese":"公園や図書館などに行きました。","reading":"こうえんやとしょかんなどにいきました。","english":"I went to places like the park and library."}]

title: 〜前に (before doing)
pattern: V (plain non-past) + 前に / N の 前に
meaning: Before doing / before [noun]
explanation: 前に after a plain non-past verb means 'before doing that action'. After a noun + の, it means 'before [that thing]'. The verb before 前に does not change tense regardless of when the event happens.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"寝る前に歯を磨いてください。","reading":"ねるまえにはをみがいてください。","english":"Please brush your teeth before sleeping."},{"japanese":"食事の前に手を洗います。","reading":"しょくじのまえにてをあらいます。","english":"I wash my hands before meals."},{"japanese":"日本に来る前に日本語を勉強しました。","reading":"にほんにくるまえににほんごをべんきょうしました。","english":"I studied Japanese before coming to Japan."}]

title: 〜後で (after doing)
pattern: V-た + 後で / N の 後で
meaning: After doing — marks a completed action that precedes another
explanation: 後で after a past-tense verb (た-form) means 'after doing'. It implies the first action is fully complete before the second begins, contrasting with てから which has a stronger sequential/causal connection.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"宿題をした後で、遊びます。","reading":"しゅくだいをしたあとで、あそびます。","english":"After doing homework, I will play."},{"japanese":"食事の後で、コーヒーを飲みます。","reading":"しょくじのあとで、コーヒーをのみます。","english":"I drink coffee after meals."},{"japanese":"仕事の後でどこかに行きますか。","reading":"しごとのあとでどこかにいきますか。","english":"Are you going somewhere after work?"}]

title: Question words (いつ / どこ / だれ / なに)
pattern: いつ / どこ / だれ / なに + V/Adj/です
meaning: When / where / who / what — the core question words
explanation: Japanese question words do not move to the front of the sentence the way English 'wh-words' do. They stay in the same position the answer would occupy. か at the end marks it as a question.
examplesJson: [{"japanese":"誕生日はいつですか。","reading":"たんじょうびはいつですか。","english":"When is your birthday?"},{"japanese":"お手洗いはどこですか。","reading":"おてあらいはどこですか。","english":"Where is the restroom?"},{"japanese":"それは何ですか。","reading":"それはなんですか。","english":"What is that?"}]
